entries.tutorialTitle: Biosecurity Principles for Smallholders
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Quarantine, disinfection, and what to do if you suspect a notifiable disease

entries.tutorialTitle: Goat housing and bedding management
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Dry, draught-free housing, correct bedding depth, and a mucking-out schedule that keeps goats healthy.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Goats need dry housing more urgently than most small livestock. They have little tolerance for sustained damp and will develop respiratory problems if ventilation is poor or bedding is left too long. This tutorial covers minimum space requirements, how to achieve good ventilation without draughts, and how often to muck out to maintain a healthy floor.

entries.tutorialTitle: Home milking a dairy goat
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Milking stand setup, udder preparation, hand-milking technique, stripping, teat dipping, and safe milk storage.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Milking a goat by hand is a learnable skill that takes most beginners a week or two to get consistent. The technique matters less than the routine: the same sequence every milking, carried out calmly, produces more milk and fewer mastitis problems than an erratic approach. This tutorial covers everything from the milking stand to storing the milk safely.

entries.tutorialTitle: Harvesting honey: uncapping and extraction
entries.tutorialSubtitle: How to remove supers, uncap the comb, and extract honey by centrifuge.

entries.tutorialTitle: Introducing a bought mated queen
entries.tutorialSubtitle: How to safely introduce a new queen using a candy-plug introduction cage.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Requeening with a bought mated queen is faster than raising your own and lets you introduce known genetics. The colony will usually reject a foreign queen placed directly on the frames. A slow release through a candy-plug cage gives the bees time to accept her pheromone before she is free.

entries.tutorialTitle: Managing sheep in winter housing
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Space, bedding, feeding, and daily checks for late-pregnancy ewes housed through the cold months.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Housing late-pregnancy ewes protects them from cold, wet, and poaching on winter pasture. But a badly managed shed creates its own problems: respiratory disease, footrot, and pregnancy toxaemia all increase when ewes are crowded or underfed. This tutorial covers the practical requirements for a safe indoor system from housing to turnout.

entries.tutorialTitle: Summer pig management: shade and cooling
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Preventing heat stress in outdoor pigs during hot weather.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Pigs cannot sweat. They lose heat only through their skin surface and breathing, which makes them highly vulnerable to heat stress above 25 degrees Celsius. A pig in heat stress stops eating, pants heavily, and lies stretched out and flat. Prolonged heat stress causes death. Shade, wallows, and adjusted feeding times are the three tools that prevent it.
